votes.reason: The Python code correctly generates all prefixes of the string using list comprehension and slicing, matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly constructs the list of all prefixes using list comprehension and string slicing, exactly matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly generates all prefixes using string slicing and compares it to the given list, matching the Coq specification exactly.

The Python code computes the same list of prefixes as the Coq spec. In Coq, `seq 0 (String.length s)` generates [0, 1, ..., len(s)-1], and `substring 0 (i+1) s` extracts the first i+1 characters. In Python, `range(len(s))` generates the same indices, and `s[:i+1]` extracts the first i+1 characters. Both produce the list of all non-empty prefixes of s in order of increasing length, and the comparison checks equality of this list with the given prefixes.

Both produce the list of prefixes [first 1 char, first 2 chars, ..., full string] and check equality with the given list; handling of empty strings and lengths matches.
Both construct the list of prefixes of lengths 1..len(s) and compare it to the given list; edge cases like empty strings align.
Both compute the list of prefixes of lengths 1..len(s) by mapping i in 0..len(s)-1 to s[0:i+1], and the Python returns True iff the given list equals that expected list.
